attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old man presents with a 2 month history of worsening fatigue, night sweats, and weight loss. His medical history includes an orthotopic liver transplant 19 years ago for a single 3 cm hepatocellular carcinoma on a background of Child-Pugh A cirrhosis. He takes cyclosporine as lifelong immunosuppressive therapy. On examination, the supraclavicular lymph nodes are enlarged, non-tender, and soft. His abdomen is uniformly distended and dull to percussion. The area of dullness changes when he is rolled onto his side.

Blood tests showed:

  • Hb 128 g/L (125–185)

  • WCC 15.1 x 10^9/L (4.5 - 11.0)

  • ESR 42 mm/hr (<20)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Whilst all the listed possible diagnoses need to be considered, the finding of enlarged supraclavicular nodes is most suggestive of underlying lymphoma. The fatigue, night sweats and weight loss comprise 'B symptoms' and on a background of immunosuppressive therapy point strongly to this diagnosis. Together with the physical findings of lymph node enlargement, and ascites, plus the laboratory findings of raised WCC, and elevated ESR, the diagnosis of lymphoma must be the first condition to exclude.

This specific form of lymphoma is classified as post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (PTLD) which is a diffuse large B-cell lymphoma by morphology. There is an increasing incidence of PTLD in developed countries, due to the increasing prevalence of organ transplants and increased use of immunosuppressive therapy. Other important risk factors include infection, race, family history and lifestyle.

While hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) recurrence is a consideration given the patient's history of liver transplant, the presence of enlarged supraclavicular lymph nodes is not a typical feature of HCC recurrence. The lymph nodes that are commonly affected in HCC are the regional lymph nodes around the liver, particularly those in the porta hepatis. Additionally, HCC may spread to other abdominal lymph nodes, such as retroperitoneal lymph nodes.

Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis is unlikely as the symptoms of fatigue, night sweats, and weight loss are not typical for SBP. Moreover, SBP is associated with ascites and infection within the peritoneal cavity, resulting in abdominal pain, tenderness and fever. The patient's abdominal distension is more likely related to a malignant process.

Chronic rejection after liver transplantation usually manifests as progressive deterioration in liver function rather than the systemic symptoms described. The presence of supraclavicular lymphadenopathy and constitutional symptoms is not characteristic of chronic rejection.

Autoimmune hepatitis is less likely, as the patient's symptoms and findings, such as supraclavicular lymphadenopathy and abdominal distension, are not typical of autoimmune hepatitis. The typical features of autoimmune hepatitis include fatigue, jaundice, abdominal discomfort, joint pain, pruritus (itching), dark urine, pale-colored stools etc.
learningPoints: Painless, palpable supraclavicular lymph nodes must always raise the possibility of a malignant process.
references: Armitage JO, Gascoyne RD, Lunning MA, et al. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Lancet 2017; 390:298-310.
Papaconstantinou D, Tsilimigras DI, Pawlik TM. Recurrent Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Patterns, Detection, Staging and Treatment. J Hepatocell Carcinoma. 2022;9:947-957.

Fijten GH, Blijham GH. Unexplained lymphadenopathy in family practice. An evaluation of the probability of malignant causes and the effectiveness of physicians' workup. J Fam Pract. 1988;27(4):373-376.

Koo JY, Kadonaga JN, Wintroub BV, Lazada-Nur FI.
The development of B-cell lymphoma in a patient with psoriasis treated with cyclosporine. J Am Acad Dermatol. 1992; 26: 836-840.
